title: Dog Hyperactivity and Inability to Settle | Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog unable to relax? Learn what causes canine hyperactivity, when to seek veterinary advice, and why tracking behavior helps triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Dog hyperactivity is typically a behavioral issue caused by lack of stimulation, but sudden restlessness can sometimes indicate underlying medical problems like hyperthyroidism or pain. While usually not an emergency, capturing a video of the behavior can help your veterinarian determine if the cause is physical or environmental.

What is hyperactivity in dogs and what causes it?

Hyperactivity in dogs, often characterized by an inability to settle or stay still, is a state where a pet remains in a constant loop of movement and distraction. While it is often behavioral—stemming from a lack of physical or mental stimulation—it can sometimes be linked to medical issues such as hyperthyroidism, allergies, or chronic pain that makes resting uncomfortable.

Is my dog's hyperactivity a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Low. In the vast majority of cases, a dog who cannot settle is not experiencing an immediate life-threatening emergency.
  • When to seek care: If restlessness is a sudden change or is accompanied by heavy panting and pacing without purpose.
  • Next steps: Schedule a non-emergency appointment with your veterinarian to rule out discomfort or neurological concerns.

How can a photo or video of my dog help with triage?

  • Differentiates behavior: A short video allows a vet to distinguish between high-energy playfulness and involuntary restlessness or anxiety-driven pacing.
  • Identifies body language: Seeing your dog's tail carriage, ear position, and focal point helps the professional assess their state of mind.
  • Determines the cause: Visual evidence helps determine if the issue is rooted in behavioral, environmental, or physical factors.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Although poorly documented in dogs, hyperactivity or attention deficit disorder can manifest as excessive motor activity and an altered ability to acquire new tasks, sometimes accompanied by stereotypic behaviors. These dogs may be particularly difficult to train to behaviorally settle and may exhibit signs of sympathetic activity even at rest, such as increased heart and respiratory rate and vasodilation. Affected dogs may respond to treatment with methylphenidate, starting with an initial dose of 0.25-0.5 mg/kg, bid, gradually increasing every few days to a maximum of 2 mg/kg until a measurable therapeutic response is seen, such as reduced motor activity and heart rate.

Chapter: Neurology, Behavioral

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1567)

faqs.question: Can certain foods make my dog hyperactive?
faqs.answer: Yes, diets high in sugar or certain fillers can lead to energy spikes. Some dogs may also react to specific proteins or additives that cause restlessness.

faqs.question: Is hyperactivity more common in specific breeds?
faqs.answer: Yes, working and herding breeds like Border Collies and Belgian Malinois have naturally higher energy levels and require more mental engagement to settle.

faqs.question: Could my dog be hyperactive because they are in pain?
faqs.answer: It is possible. Some dogs pace or refuse to lie down because they are experiencing joint pain or abdominal discomfort and cannot find a comfortable position.
